question_text: Module 3 opens by defining salvation in a simple way. According to the text, salvation is best described as which of the following?
answer_options: The helpless and dying receiving help to live again
A reward given to those who try their hardest to obey God
A moral improvement that good people gradually achieve
An inspiring message that motivates people to do better
correct_answer: The helpless and dying receiving help to live again

question_text: The lesson defines salvation by grace using a picture of a gift. How does the text describe salvation by grace?
answer_options: Receiving the gift of life that you could not buy or afford
Earning life by paying God back over time
A loan of life that must be repaid through good works
A wage given to man for his good conduct
correct_answer: Receiving the gift of life that you could not buy or afford

question_text: The text uses the image of a drowning man to make a point about the sinner's deepest need. What does it say the sinner first needs?
answer_options: Salvation, rather than inspiration, religion, or moral polishing
Better advice on how to fix his own life
A religion that gives him rules to follow
Moral polishing to slowly become acceptable
correct_answer: Salvation, rather than inspiration, religion, or moral polishing

question_text: In the section on Repentance and Faith, the text distinguishes the two by direction. How does it describe them?
answer_options: Repentance turns away from sin, and faith turns toward Jesus Christ
Repentance turns toward Christ, and faith turns away from sin
Repentance and faith both mean simply feeling sorry for sin
Repentance is receiving Christ, and faith is changing one's mind
correct_answer: Repentance turns away from sin, and faith turns toward Jesus Christ

question_text: The section on the New Birth contrasts forgiveness with something more. According to the text, what does God do beyond cancelling a debt and removing guilt?
answer_options: He gives new life and regenerates the inner man
He leaves the man the same but excused
He gives the man a second chance to try harder
He removes only the penalty but not the guilt
correct_answer: He gives new life and regenerates the inner man

question_text: The text describes 'the Great Exchange' that took place at the cross. What does it say was exchanged?
answer_options: Your debt became His, and His righteousness became yours
Your good works became the basis of your acceptance
Jesus sympathized with man's pain without taking his place
Man's effort was exchanged for God's approval
correct_answer: Your debt became His, and His righteousness became yours

question_text: The lesson defines eternal life in a way that goes beyond mere length of existence. How does the text describe eternal life?
answer_options: The very life of God shared with the believer, beginning now and continuing forever
Only an endless existence that begins after a person dies
Merely a better future that the believer is still waiting to receive
A reward earned by faithful living over a lifetime
correct_answer: The very life of God shared with the believer, beginning now and continuing forever

question_text: The text is careful about how commitment relates to salvation. According to the lesson, what is commitment?
answer_options: The fruit of a life that has truly encountered salvation
The price a man pays in order to be saved
Careless living that grace permits after salvation
The works that earn a man his right standing with God
correct_answer: The fruit of a life that has truly encountered salvation

question_text: In 'The First Walk of the Saved Man,' the text uses a door-and-road image to explain salvation and discipleship. Which statement matches the text?
answer_options: Salvation is the door, but discipleship is the road
Discipleship is the door, but salvation is the road
Salvation is both the door and the end of the journey
A single decision is all the New Testament pattern requires
correct_answer: Salvation is the door, but discipleship is the road

question_text: The 'What Changed' summary ends with a set of contrasts capturing the whole lesson. Which set of contrasts does the text give?
answer_options: Not improved but reborn; not tolerated but accepted in Christ; not self-made but grace-made; not left alone but called to follow
Not forgiven but ignored; not rescued but advised; not reborn but improved; not led but left alone
Not saved by grace but earned by works; not accepted but tolerated; not reborn but excused
Not a gift but a salary; not a turning but mere tears; not new life but a second chance
correct_answer: Not improved but reborn; not tolerated but accepted in Christ; not self-made but grace-made; not left alone but called to follow
